primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected acute coronary syndrome (unstable angina/evolving MI) from premature coronary artery disease, on a background of probable familial hypercholesterolaemia

pathophysiology_summary: Familial hypercholesterolaemia (FH) is an inherited disorder of LDL-receptor function causing lifelong markedly elevated LDL cholesterol and, consequently, very premature and accelerated atherosclerotic coronary artery disease. Chronic LDL elevation deposits cholesterol not only in coronary arteries but in tendons (Achilles tendon xanthomas), eyelids (xanthelasma) and the corneal periphery (corneal arcus appearing at a young age) - physical stigmata that betray the underlying disorder. In this 34-year-old with a strong family history of premature myocardial infarction, the acute presentation is exertional, jaw-radiating chest tightness that only partly settles with a residual ache, on a crescendo two-week background - a pattern of unstable angina/acute coronary syndrome caused by an unstable/ruptured atherosclerotic plaque with superimposed thrombus limiting coronary flow. The immediate danger is progression to complete coronary occlusion (ST-elevation MI), malignant arrhythmia and sudden cardiac death. The immediate management priority is therefore the possible ACS - aspirin, an ECG, troponin and continuous monitoring, and emergency cardiology assessment - not the chronic lipid disorder, which is managed later with aggressive lifelong lipid-lowering therapy. ECG, troponin and monitoring cannot be done via telehealth, so the correct action is to call an ambulance immediately.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe exertional crushing chest tightness that only partly settles, with residual ache, is possible ACS from the first turn and must be treated as such.
Carrying groceries could suggest a muscular strain, but exertional, jaw-radiating, crushing pain is not typical of a musculoskeletal cause.
Can cause chest discomfort, but onset tied to exertion rather than meals/lying down points away from reflux.
The patient is anxious, but the exertional trigger and jaw radiation indicate an organic ischaemic cause rather than panic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the outset the AI Doctor must treat this as possible ACS regardless of the patient's youth and fitness, and not anchor on benign musculoskeletal/reflux/anxiety explanations.

differential.diagnosis: Acute coronary syndrome (unstable angina/evolving MI) from premature CAD
differential.evidence_basis: Exertional crushing chest tightness (DI-001) + jaw radiation (DI-002) + two-week crescendo pattern (DI-003) + strong family history of premature MI (DI-004) together strongly indicate ACS.
Exertional, jaw-radiating, crushing pain with a crescendo pattern is not musculoskeletal.
Exertional onset is not consistent with reflux.
The organic exertional ischaemic features override anxiety as the explanation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the AI Doctor should recognise probable ACS requiring an immediate ambulance, aspirin and ECG/troponin - never a next-day GP appointment.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected acute coronary syndrome from premature CAD (probable underlying familial hypercholesterolaemia) - requiring emergency assessment
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. ACS cannot be confirmed/excluded remotely; ECG, troponin and monitoring are required, so an immediate emergency response is the only appropriate action. The FH is confirmed later with lipids/genetics.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is possible acute coronary syndrome needing an immediate ambulance and aspirin - the underlying familial hypercholesterolaemia is a later outpatient diagnosis, not the acute priority.

red_flags.description: Severe exertional crushing central chest tightness with only partial relief on rest and a residual ache
red_flags.clinical_significance: Exertional chest pain that does not fully resolve is a hallmark of unstable angina/ACS and requires emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissed as strain/reflux; an evolving MI progresses to complete occlusion, arrhythmia or death.

red_flags.description: Radiation of the chest pain to the left jaw
red_flags.clinical_significance: Radiation to the jaw is a classic feature of cardiac ischaemic pain, increasing the probability of ACS.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A key ischaemic feature is overlooked and the ACS under-triaged.

red_flags.description: Two-week crescendo pattern of new, increasingly easily provoked angina
red_flags.clinical_significance: A crescendo pattern indicates unstable angina - an acute coronary syndrome that can progress to infarction imminently.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: The unstable trajectory is missed and intervention delayed.

red_flags.description: Strong family history of premature MI/sudden cardiac death and markedly high cholesterol (father died young)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A powerful risk factor for premature coronary disease that raises the pre-test probability of ACS and points to familial hypercholesterolaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Age-anchoring without weighting the family history leads to dangerous under-triage.

red_flags.description: Achilles tendon xanthomas, xanthelasma and corneal arcus at a young age on examination (stigmata of familial hypercholesterolaemia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited stigmata indicate severe genetic hyperlipidaemia driving premature coronary disease; the palpated tendon xanthomas in particular are examination findings.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - these are examination findings; the acute action remains emergency ACS assessment.

red_flags.description: Ischaemic ECG changes and/or elevated troponin on emergency assessment - pending
red_flags.clinical_significance: ECG and troponin are required to diagnose and risk-stratify ACS and cannot be obtained via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires monitored emergency assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Age-anchoring ('too young for a heart attack')
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The patient's youth and fitness can lead the AI Doctor to dismiss cardiac causes, ignoring that familial hypercholesterolaemia causes very premature coronary disease.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Attributing exertional pain to a muscle strain from carrying groceries
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The exertional trigger can be misread as musculoskeletal; crushing, jaw-radiating, incompletely-resolving pain is ischaemic until proven otherwise.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Labelling the presentation as a panic attack
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anxiety is present, but attributing the pain to panic risks missing an evolving MI.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Deferring to a next-day GP or self-driving
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising the patient to wait or drive himself in delays emergency care for a possible evolving MI and risks collapse en route.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High risk on history alone (typical exertional pain, strong risk factors, age notwithstanding) - ECG and troponin required to complete risk stratification
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A high-risk history mandates emergency ambulance transfer for ECG, troponin and cardiology assessment; risk cannot be down-rated by youth.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Cardiac auscultation and full cardiovascular examination
Fasting lipid profile and genetic testing to confirm familial hypercholesterolaemia
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for an immediate ambulance; advise the patient to stop all exertion, sit and rest, and chew a 300 mg aspirin unless contraindicated (allergy/bleeding), while awaiting paramedics for ECG, troponin and continuous monitoring.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring a fit 34-year-old that this is muscular, reflux or anxiety, or advising a next-day GP visit, risks a fatal evolving myocardial infarction.
